rationale: In the citing paper, Dawar and Pillutla (2000) is cited in the problem framing of product-harm crises and again in the focused review of prior crisis and negative-publicity research. The authors explicitly position their study as extending/complementing that prior stream by modeling consumers as Bayesian updaters and using longitudinal scanner data. This is more than a passing mention because the citation helps structure the paper's conceptual motivation and contribution claim, even though the empirical method and outcomes differ.
relation_description: The 2011 paper uses Dawar and Pillutla (2000) as key prior evidence on product-harm crisis effects and as conceptual grounding for belief-updating in crises. It then develops a different empirical approach, a dynamic scanner-based choice model, to build on that foundation.
